old_claim.note: Public Archives provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Vindonissa field infirmary display includes herbs and ointments alongside instruments and prescriptions, so prepared medicinal materials are part of the stocked Roman medical-supply setting.

source_shape.source_locator: Museum Aargau describes a fully equipped reconstructed field infirmary at Vindonissa and lists surgical instruments, herbs, ointments, prescription scrolls, and camp beds.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source places prepared medical materials inside the Roman military infirmary supply setting.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The prior public-archives edge made documentary repositories the mechanism; the museum source instead supports medical materials and prepared remedies as the concrete stocked contents.

ontology_before: State Medical Supply Stores depended on Public Archives, implying public record repositories enabled medical supply storage.
ontology_after: Roman Military Medical Supply Stores is scoped to military infirmary provisioning, with prepared herbal medicines as a concrete stocked material and no direct public-archives dependency.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: state_medical_supply_stores now depends on herbal_medicine_preparation and no longer directly depends on public_archives.
would_reject_if: A source demonstrates that the earliest scoped Roman military medical supply stores were primarily archival offices.
The node is later rescoped to generic civic archive stores rather than medical supplies in military infirmaries.
A separate prescription_archive node is added and should carry a public_archives or record_keeping edge.
short_reason: The source-backed mechanism is stocked medical materials in a Roman infirmary, not public archival infrastructure.
